title: The Foundations Ladder
excerpt: Six rungs from the ground up: what data is, what analysis does, and where analytics actually sits. Built and cold-walked in one session.

questions.prompt: At the very bottom of the ladder: what is data?
options: A chart or graph that explains something
Any conclusion you draw from research
questions.explanation: Data = a collection of facts. One fact is a single true thing ("it rained Tuesday"); pile facts together and you have data. A chart is an artifact built FROM data, not data itself.
questions.hint: It's the rawest thing on the ladder — before any chart, conclusion, or insight exists.

questions.explanation: Analysis turns data into INSIGHTS — the understanding itself ("long hiring processes cause new hires to quit"). An artifact (a chart, a report) can carry an insight, but the insight is the point. Decisions come after the insight.
questions.hint: An artifact only carries it. The thing itself is the understanding.

questions.prompt: The everyday mechanism of analysis, in order:
questions.explanation: Pattern → Relationship → Prediction → Action. The first move is spotting a PATTERN — not Process (that lives up in the six-step lifecycle). Watch the two-P trap.
questions.hint: Pure Reform Pushes Action. First P = Pattern, NOT Process.

questions.prompt: Data science is the umbrella. Which three disciplines sit under it?
questions.explanation: Data science = "the discipline of making data useful," with three children: Statistics, Machine Learning/AI, and Analytics. (LLMs aren't a co-equal bucket — they're one tool inside ML.)
questions.hint: One of them is the bucket that an LLM lives INSIDE of.

questions.prompt: The three are split by how many decisions you know you want to make up front. Which one is "I don't know yet — I'm exploring"?
questions.explanation: Analytics = you DON'T know how many decisions yet; you're exploring for inspiration, hunting unknown unknowns. The analyst is the explorer/detective. (Statistics = a FEW known decisions; ML = MANY, automated.)
questions.hint: Explorer = don't-know. "Here's data nobody's looked at — go find something."

questions.prompt: In Cassie's split, statistics is for making ___ decisions under uncertainty.
questions.explanation: Statistics = a FEW important decisions under uncertainty, made carefully (its excellence is rigor — the careful philosopher). Many automated decisions = Machine Learning.
questions.hint: The careful philosopher protecting a small number of high-stakes calls.

questions.prompt: In Ask → Prepare → Process → Analyze → Share → Act, which step comes immediately BEFORE Process?
questions.explanation: Prepare comes before Process — you plan (Prepare) before you clean (Process). Your mnemonic encodes it: PUPPIES (Prepare) before PICK (Process).
questions.hint: All Puppies Pick Another Short Ally. PUPPIES before PICK.

questions.prompt: Which best describes the Process step (not Analyze)?
options: Clean the data and handle it ethically — consent, restrict access, store securely
Run the data through lenses to find the insights
Share the findings with managers in the right context
Define the business question and what success looks like
questions.explanation: Process = wash and prep: clean the data, get consent, restrict access, aggregate to protect individuals, store securely. Finding the insights is Analyze (the cooking). Defining the question is Ask; sharing is Share.
questions.hint: Food: Process = wash & prep the ingredients. Analyze = cook.

questions.explanation: Descriptive = what happened · Diagnostic = WHY it happened · Predictive = what will happen · Prescriptive = what should we do. They climb: what → why → what-next → what-to-do.
questions.hint: It's the doctor's word — diagnosing the cause.

questions.prompt: You want the analysis to recommend the best action to take. Which type?
questions.explanation: Prescriptive = what should we do — it prescribes the best action (like a prescription). Predictive only forecasts what will happen; it doesn't tell you what to do about it.
questions.hint: Like a prescription from a doctor — it tells you what to DO.

questions.prompt: In your own words: what does data analysis do to data, and what's the difference between an insight and an artifact?
questions.answer: Data analysis turns data (a collection of facts) into INSIGHTS — the understanding itself, e.g. "long, complicated hiring makes new hires quit." An ARTIFACT (a chart, a report) is just a thing that carries the insight; the insight is the point, the artifact is the delivery.
questions.explanation: The key distinction you sharpened in E27: analysis produces understanding (insight), not just objects (artifacts). Artifacts only carry insights.
questions.hint: One is the understanding itself; the other just carries it.

questions.prompt: From memory, name the six steps of the data analysis process — in order.
questions.answer: Ask → Prepare → Process → Analyze → Share → Act
questions.explanation: The spine of the whole certificate. Prepare before Process (plan before clean); Analyze before Share before Act.
questions.hint: All Puppies Pick Another Short Ally.

questions.prompt: Explain what separates statistics, machine learning, and analytics in Cassie's map.
questions.answer: How many decisions you know you want to make before you begin. Statistics = a FEW important decisions under uncertainty (rigor). Machine learning = MANY decisions, automated, under uncertainty (performance). Analytics = you DON'T know how many yet — you're exploring for inspiration (speed). All three sit under the data-science umbrella.
questions.explanation: The dividing axis is the number of decisions known up front — the framing that finally untied the analytics-vs-science knot.
questions.hint: It's about how many decisions you know about up front.
